planting.note: Plant container-grown trees or rooted cuttings in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. White Indian Guava is slightly more cold-hardy than pink-fleshed types, tolerating brief temperatures to about 26°F. In zone 9b–10 it fruits reliably year-round with peaks in summer and winter. Protect young trees from freezes below 28°F with frost cloth in zone 9a.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile soil — guavas tolerate a wide range of soil types including sandy and slightly alkaline soils; White Indian Guava is a white-to-cream-fleshed cultivar of common guava producing round to oval fruits with yellow skin when ripe, white to pale-cream flesh, and a sweeter, milder, less musky flavor than pink-fleshed guava varieties — the gentler flavor makes White Indian more approachable for people who find the distinctive strong fragrance of pink guava overpowering. The fruits have very few, soft seeds and a texture similar to a ripe pear. White-fleshed guavas are preferred in some Asian cuisines where they are eaten fresh with salt and chili or blended into drinks; they also make exceptional guava paste, jelly, and guava cheese when cooked. The trees are prolific once established and produce two or more crops annually in zone 9 and 10. Thin fruits to 4–6 inches apart to encourage larger fruit development.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Guava Varieties for Florida
California Rare Fruit Growers Association — Psidium guajava Cultivars
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Guava Production for South Texas

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees or rooted cuttings in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. Red Malaysian Guava has moderate cold tolerance to about 28°F for brief periods when established. The deeply red fruit color is most intense in slightly cooler temperatures — fall and winter fruits in zone 9 often color more deeply than summer fruits. Protect young trees from freezes in zone 9a with frost cloth.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile soil; Red Malaysian Guava is a striking cultivar producing fruits with a red-blushed skin and deep salmon-pink to red flesh — the most colorful of the common guava cultivars, with a vibrant internal color that is dramatically vivid when the fruit is sliced. The flavor is rich, aromatic, and sweet with the characteristic guava fragrance, perhaps slightly less musky than standard pink guava types. The red flesh color is due to lycopene, the same antioxidant pigment as in tomatoes, and red-fleshed guavas contain significantly more lycopene than pink-fleshed varieties. Red Malaysian guava is visually impressive at the market and on the table — the vivid red cross-section makes it one of the most photographed fresh tropical fruits. In zone 9 the trees produce multiple crops annually and fruit most heavily in late summer–fall and late winter–spring. Fruits ripen from green to a yellow-red skin color and should be eaten when fully soft.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Red-Fleshed Guava Varieties
California Rare Fruit Growers Association — Guava Cultivar Comparison
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Guava Production Notes

planting.note: Root hardwood cuttings in late winter or plant container-grown trees in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. Verte de Bordeaux is a productive, compact-to-medium fig tree well-suited to zone 9 conditions. It produces two crops in zone 9: a breba crop on old wood in June–July and a main crop on new wood in August–September. Prune lightly after harvest to maintain size and encourage productive new wood.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil — figs produce best in lean conditions and can become overly vegetative in rich, heavily fertilized soil; Verte de Bordeaux is a French heirloom fig from the Bordeaux region of southwestern France, producing small to medium, pear-shaped fruits with green to greenish-yellow skin that remains green even when fully ripe — a distinctive appearance that surprises consumers expecting a ripe fig to be darker. The deep burgundy-red interior flesh has an intensely rich, wine-like, complex sweetness that fig connoisseurs often rate among the finest flavors in the Ficus carica species. The petite size (1–2 inches) and striking green skin with deeply colored interior make it a specialty market favorite. In zone 9 Verte de Bordeaux produces consistently and handles the heat and humidity of Gulf Coast summers well. The green skin can be confusing to novice growers who may pick the fruit too early — harvest when the neck of the fig bends and the fruit softens.
sources: California Rare Fruit Growers Association — French Fig Cultivars
University of California Cooperative Extension — Fig Variety Trial Results
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Figs for Louisiana Gardens

planting.note: Root hardwood cuttings in late winter or plant container-grown trees in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. Croisic is primarily valued for its breba crop (early summer figs on previous season's wood); in zone 9 the first breba figs ripen in late May–June, providing some of the earliest figs of the season. The main crop matures August–September.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Croisic is a French fig variety named for the coastal commune of Le Croisic in Brittany, notable primarily for its prolific breba crop — the early-season figs produced on previous year's wood that ripen in late May to early June in zone 9, weeks ahead of the main crop figs on most other varieties. The early-ripening breba figs are medium to large, with a yellow-green to yellow skin and amber to orange-tinged flesh with a mild, honey-sweet flavor. In zone 9 the Croisic fig provides the earliest fresh fig harvest of the season and is particularly valued by gardeners who want to extend the fig-eating season by starting early. The main crop figs on new wood ripen in August–September and are similar in quality to the breba crop. In zone 9 the mild winters and long growing season allow Croisic to produce heavy breba crops — the consistently warm temperatures maintain the tree's vigor for prolific breba production.
sources: Louisiana State University AgCenter — Fig Cultivar Selection for Louisiana
California Rare Fruit Growers Association — Early-Ripening Fig Varieties
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Figs for Florida

planting.note: Root hardwood cuttings in late winter or plant container-grown trees in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. Ronde de Bordeaux is a compact-to-medium fig tree well-suited to container growing and small gardens. In zone 9 it produces a breba crop in June–July and a main crop in August–October. The compact habit makes it one of the better figs for container growing in zone 9.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Ronde de Bordeaux (Round Bordeaux) is a French heirloom fig from the Bordeaux region producing small, round, dark-purple to nearly black-skinned fruits with brilliant deep red to garnet-colored flesh — one of the most richly colored of all fig varieties. The dark skin and brilliant interior make Ronde de Bordeaux a spectacular fresh eating fig and an equally attractive fig for jam and preserves where the deep color produces richly-hued products. The flavor is very sweet, rich, and slightly winey with the complexity associated with dark-skinned fig varieties. The compact tree habit makes Ronde de Bordeaux one of the most practical figs for home gardens and container growing — it produces prolifically despite its modest size. It is an early-maturing variety with the main crop ripening in late July–August in zone 9, among the earliest of the dark-skinned European fig types. This fig has become very popular among fig enthusiasts in zone 9 and zone 10 for its combination of early ripening, outstanding flavor, and manageable tree size.
sources: California Rare Fruit Growers Association — Ronde de Bordeaux Fig
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Dark-Skinned Fig Varieties
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Figs for Florida Gardens
